title: B2B Sales Engine Readiness

introduction.subtitle: Revenue maturity assessment. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
B2B Sales Engine Readiness helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind ICP & Qualification, Process & Conversion, Enablement & Customer Handoff, Forecasting & Retention. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How precisely are target accounts and non-fit accounts defined?

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in ICP & Qualification; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in ICP & Qualification, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. ICP & Qualification has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. ICP & Qualification is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: ICP & Qualification: How precisely are target accounts and non-fit accounts defined
conceptHelp.summary: ICP & Qualification means defining which accounts are worth selling to and which opportunities deserve sales time, using fit, urgency, budget, authority, and use case. This question narrows the concept to "How precisely are target accounts and non-fit accounts defined?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: ICP & Qualification matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "How precisely are target accounts and non-fit accounts defined?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

options.text: A. The answer is broad, assumed, or based mostly on internal belief.
B. There is a high-level description, but segmentation, trigger, or buyer evidence is thin.
C. The answer is defined for a specific segment with documented behaviour, alternatives, and decision criteria.
D. The answer is validated across repeated cases and directly informs targeting, messaging, prioritisation, and trade-offs.

conceptHelp.title: Process & Conversion: How objective are sales stages

questions.text: How repeatable are discovery, demonstration, and proof?

options.text: A. The answer relies mainly on opinion, pitch, memory, or isolated examples.
B. There are supporting examples, but sources are partial, informal, or hard to compare.
C. There is documented evidence from relevant customers, operations, metrics, or records.
D. The evidence is triangulated, current, owner-reviewed, and strong enough to change a material decision.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Enablement & Customer Handoff: How repeatable are discovery, demonstration, and proof
conceptHelp.summary: Enablement & Customer Handoff means making discovery, demos, proof, onboarding, and customer-success transfer repeatable enough that growth does not depend on one hero seller. This question narrows the concept to "How repeatable are discovery, demonstration, and proof?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

conceptHelp.title: Forecasting & Retention: How evidence-based is the forecast

questions.text: How quickly can sales disqualify attractive but wrong-fit opportunities?

options.text: A. No concrete example shows disqualifying wrong-fit deals; the answer relies on intention, memory, or individual judgement.
B. There are isolated examples of disqualifying wrong-fit deals, but they are inconsistent, hard to verify, or depend on one person.
C. Recent examples show disqualifying wrong-fit deals in practice, with enough detail to judge quality and repeatability.
D. Disqualifying wrong-fit deals is repeatedly demonstrated under pressure and changes decisions, ownership, or trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: ICP & Qualification: How quickly can sales disqualify attractive but wrong-fit opportunities
conceptHelp.summary: ICP & Qualification is tested here through a concrete operating question: "How quickly can sales disqualify attractive but wrong-fit opportunities?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: ICP & Qualification matters because weak practice in this area can make the whole result look more mature than the operating system really is.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable behaviour: artifacts, decisions, customer evidence, owners, reviews, or shipped work. Do not score intention as practice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
